coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent and visual detection of methyl-paraoxon by using boron-and nitrogen-doped carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Luo, Mai
Wei, Jinchao
Zhao, Yunyang
Sun, Yuzhou
Liang, Hengxing
Wang, Shuangpeng
Li, Peng
coredata.dc:description: Methyl-paraoxon (MP) is a representative organophosphorus pesticide (OP), which is a huge threat to the safety of human beings. In this study, carbon dots doped with boron and nitrogen (B, N–CDs) were used as a fluorescent probe for MP detection. The prepared cerium oxide (CeO2) decompose MP efficiently into p-nitrophenol (p-NP) and phosphate group in the alkaline condition. The dissociative p-NP could reduce the fluorescent intensity of B, N–CDs through the inner filter effect (IFE). The pH value, incubation time, the amount of B, N–CDs and solvent effect were investigated and optimized for detection system. Under the optimum conditions, the limit of the detection was calculated to be 24.7 ng mL−1 with simple procedures. Tap water, lake water, Semen Coicis and Panax quinquefolius L. samples were used to test the recoveries of the detection system. The recoveries ranged from 94.68% to 107.28%. Furthermore, the fluorescence laminate was developed by painting B, N–CDs on the polyamide laminate. The MP residue could be observed directly under the 365 nm lamp, and linearity for MP by using the fluorescent laminates was ranged from 0.015 to 1.5 mM. The present work provides a rapid and dual-signal based method (fluorescent and visual) for routine detection of MP in real samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Methyl-paraoxon
Carbon dots
Fluorescent probe
Cerium oxide
Fluorescence laminates

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Visual detection of MP by B, N–CDs fluorescent polyamide laminate

GuiKe AA18118049 ), National Science and Technology Major Projects for “Major New Drugs Innovation and Development” (File no. 2018ZX09201018-023 ), and the Research Committee of the University of Macau (File no. MYRG2018-00239-ICMS ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterzation of B, N–CDs: High-resolution TEM image (a), IR (b) and XPS full scan spectrum (c). After peak fitting, XPS high-resolution survey scan of B, N–CDs, B 1s (d), C 1s (e) and N 1s (f).
Characterization of CeO2: TEM image (a), high-resolution TEM image of CeO2 (b), XPS full scan spectrum(c). X–RD of CeO2 spectrum with CeO2 card of JCPDS database (d).

(a) Emission spectra of B, N–CDs under different excitation wavelength. (b) The UV–vis absorption spectra of p-NP (10 μM, shadow area), fluorescence excitation (Ex.) and emission (Em.) spectra of B, N–CDs (1.18 mg mL−1).

(a) Fluorescence spectra of B, N–CDs with different concentration of MP (0–25 μM). (b) Fluorescent quenching rate of B, N–CDs with MP concentration increased from 0.1 to 25 μM. Inset: quenching rate of liner regression relationship with MP from 0.1 to 15 μM.
Fluorescent quenching rate of B, N–CDs with the addition of 15 μM MP and interference substances.

(a) B, N–CDs fluorescence laminates with CeO2 and MP at different concentration (365 nm) (I), and the laminate image was transferred into the 3D model by Image J software (II); (b) The selectivity of B, N–CDs fluorescence laminates in the presence of different pesticides (365 nm) (I) and the laminate image was transferred into the 3D model by Image J software (II).
Schematic representation of a fluorescent and visual detection method for methyl-paraoxon by using B, N–CDs.
Recoveries for MP in Semen Coicis and Panax quinquefolius L. sample.
Recoveries for MP in tap and lake water samples.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent and visual detection of methyl-paraoxon by using boron-and nitrogen-doped carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Quality Research in Chinese Medicine, Institute of Chinese Medical Sciences, University of Macau, Macau, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Methyl-paraoxon (MP) is a representative organophosphorus pesticide (OP), which is a huge threat to the safety of human beings. In this study, carbon dots doped with boron and nitrogen (B, N–CDs) were used as a fluorescent probe for MP detection. The prepared cerium oxide (CeO2) decompose MP efficiently into p-nitrophenol (p-NP) and phosphate group in the alkaline condition. The dissociative p-NP could reduce the fluorescent intensity of B, N–CDs through the inner filter effect (IFE). The pH value, incubation time, the amount of B, N–CDs and solvent effect were investigated and optimized for detection system. Under the optimum conditions, the limit of the detection was calculated to be 24.7 ng mL−1 with simple procedures. Tap water, lake water, Semen Coicis and Panax quinquefolius L. samples were used to test the recoveries of the detection system. The recoveries ranged from 94.68% to 107.28%. Furthermore, the fluorescence laminate was developed by painting B, N–CDs on the polyamide laminate. The MP residue could be observed directly under the 365 nm lamp, and linearity for MP by using the fluorescent laminates was ranged from 0.015 to 1.5 mM. The present work provides a rapid and dual-signal based method (fluorescent and visual) for routine detection of MP in real samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Organophosphorus pesticides (OPs) are extensively used in agriculture as a guard to protect crops with excellent efficacy from pests in modern commercial agriculture. OPs still take more than 30% of pesticides for sale a year in the world due to their high effectiveness and satisfactory cost performance [1,2]. However, excessive use, incorrect disposition and even low content of exposure of OPs in water, fruit, natural plants will cause critical harm to human health, especially to children [3–5]. For example, OPs leading the accumulation of acetylcholine in human body would increase the risk of Parkinson's disease and cause the other fatal consequences [6,7]. Not only do OPs have the toxicity to human beings, but also metabolites of OPs (e.g. MP) present an even more serious threat to human health [8,9]. Methyl-parathion would be oxidized to the more toxic metabolite methyl-paraoxon through the solar irradiation [10]. MP could inhibit the enzyme acetylcholinesterase (AChE, EC 3.1.1.7). The inhibition of AChE causes serious acetylcholine accumulation which increases the risk of suffering from nervous system diseases [11,12]. All in all, MP was found as a huge threat to the human being. The analytical method for MP detection with simplicity, rapidness, accuracy is necessary to be settled.

The traditional analytical methods directly sensing OPs include gas chromatography (GC), micellar electrokinetic chromatography (MEKC) and the other methods [13–15]. These methods have some advantages including low detection limit, high accuracy and selectivity. But these methods require lots of experimental pretreatment, abundant solvent waste, a lot of time consumption, and needing a good technician. So, relying on complex methods are much difficult to detect OPs frequently in a short time. Recently, many sensing techniques with high sensitivities have been developed to detect OPs, including chemiluminescent sensor, immunoassay, electrochemical sensor and enzyme-based biosensor [16–19]. But some of these methods mentioned above still have several disadvantages like bad environmental adaptability or critical environmental requirements for material storage. For this shortage, the fluorescent probe like carbon dots (CDs) is a good choice for the problems above. The fluorescent probe possesses a series of advantages like photostability, biocompatibility, environmental adaptability, being easy to decorate the surface, being highly tunable photoluminescence (PL) property and chemical inertness [20]. The most prominent advantage is that the fluorescent probe could be synthesized by various materials in green routes. The precursor compounds materials do not contain any heavy metal elements, which would greatly reduce the impact on the environment. Moreover, carbon dots are chosen because they can be easily synthesized with different wavelength [21].

The fluorescent probe is used as a highly selective and sensitive sensor based on different mechanisms. Photoinduced electron transfer (PET), Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) and inner filter effect (IFE) are the major fluorescent quenching mechanism. Some heavy metal ions and endogenous salt ions, like Pb2+, Hg2+, Co2+, 2,4-dichlorophenoxyacetic acid (2,4-D), endogenous bisulfite and peroxynitrite, could be easily detected by fluorescent probe through the PET and FRET mechanism [22–25]. Moreover, novel ratiometric fluorescent methods exhibit the great potential for a wide range of biosensing and diagnostic applications due to its self-calibration, visualization, real-time analysis, and so on [26]. The quenching efficiency of PET or FRET-based fluorescent probes is dependent on the distance of the donor and acceptor and easily to be influenced by isomers due to the analogous chemical structures. The UV absorbances of isomers would presence different spectra. For this phenomenon, the IFE-based fluorescent probe can be recognized the isomers like o-nitrophenol, m-nitrophenol and p-nitrophenol [27]. IFE is a mechanism which is between UV–vis absorption and fluorescence without energy transfer, causing the intensity change of fluorescence probe. Only Fluorescent CDs are required to cover the UV absorption of the target molecule [28]. Therefore, IFE-based fluorescent probes are potentially used in complex detection conditions or other fields. However, IFE-based fluorescent probe was easily affected by the environmental condition when the probe united the biological enzyme for MP detection. To solve this disadvantage, inorganic catalyst could be the ideal replacement to cooperate with the fluorescent probe for the target detection.

In this work, we used an inorganic catalyst “cerium oxide” combined with fluorescent carbon dots on the base of IFE mechanism for MP detection (Scheme 1). 3-aminobenzeneboronic was chosen as a precursor to calibrate the wavelength and enhance the fluorescence intensity of carbon dots [29–31]. The present synthesized carbon dots doped with boron and nitrogen, exhibited strong blue fluorescence emission at 440 nm as expected. With the addition of MP into carbon dots suspension in the presence of CeO2, fluorescence reduction happened due to IFE effect. A distinct overlap between the fluorescent spectra of CDs and the absorption spectrum of p-NP could be overserved. Inspired by the rational design and application of fluorometric visualized technique [32], a fluorescent laminates sensor was developed for visual detection of MP. It was the first time to detect MP combined CeO2 with carbon dots through fluorescence and visual detection. Finally, the proposed method has been successfully applied for MP detection in real samples including water and herbal plant samples.

UV–vis absorption spectra, IR spectra and Fluorescence spectra were observed by DR 6000 UV–vis spectrophotometer (HACH), Nicolet iS10 IR spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific) and Lumina fluorescence spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific). The polyamide laminate images were observed by ZF-7 ultraviolet analyzer (jiapeng technology Co., Ltd, Shanghai, China.). High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were measured by transmission electron microscope (FEI Talos F200X, Thermo Fisher Scientific, Massachusetts, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was measured by Escalab 250Xi (Thermo Fisher Scientific). X-ray diffraction (X-RD) patterns of CeO2 were measured by Bruker D8 advance.

3-aminobenzeneboronic acid, alanine, proline, sodium chloride, sodium sulfate, serine, leucine, ammonia, hydrogen peroxide, isopentane, dimethylformamide (DMF), sodium hydroxide, citric acid, p-nitrophenol were supplied by Aladdin reagent Co., Ltd., ShangHai, China. MP, dichlorvos, fensulfothion, paraoxon-ethyl, malaoxon and monocrotophos were supplied by Dr. Ehrenstorfer GmbH. The polyamide laminate laminates were purchased from MACHEREY-NAGEL GmbH & Co. KG, Duren, Germany.

Under the optimum conditions, a bottom-up approach for preparing B, N–CDs was carried out through the one-pot hydrothermal treatment. 1 g citric acid (CA) and 130 mg 3-aminobenzeneboronic (APBA) were dissolved in 10 mL DMF. The mixture was heated in polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) reaction kettle (20 mL) at 200 ℃ for 6 h. The brown solution was obtained after cooling to room temperature, and then dried in the vacuum oven to remove the DMF at 80 ℃ for 6 h. The precipitate was washed three times with deionized water to remove the impurities. Finally, the as-prepared B, N–CDs was dissolved in 66.7 mL DMF with 33.3 mL of 2.5 g L − 1 NaOH solution and stored at 4 ℃ for later use.

The effective vacancy-CeO2 was synthesized by one-pot hydrothermal method [33]. The synthetic method could be simply depicted as that: the first step was to dissolve cerium chloride (1.0 g) in 75 mL deionized water. The second step was to add hydrogen peroxide (30%, 2.5 mL) and ammonium (7.5 mL) into the above solution and stirred at 100 ℃ for 1 h The mixture was centrifuged to remove the upper solution and washed with deionized water until the pH was neutral. Finally, the precipitation was freeze-dried to powder and stored at 4 ℃ for later use.

10 mg CeO2, 300 μL of the as-prepared B, N–CDs solution, 100 μL MP at different concentration in Tris buffer (1 mM, pH 10) and 600 μL of water were added into 2 mL centrifuge tube to be heated at 65 ℃ for 10 min in water bath. After the samples cooling down to room temperature, it was centrifuged to remove the participate and the supernatant was transferred to cuvette for detecting the fluorescence intensity.

500 mL water sample was taken from the lake in the campus and from the tap of the student dorm room. After taking the sample, it was filtered by 0.22 μm Millipore filter to remove the solid impurity. The water samples were spiked with the different levels of MP and well mixed for later detection.

As for the herbal plant sample, 5 g was weighted into 50 mL centrifuge tube and then spiked with proper level of MP. Next, 10 mL of water was added into the tube and mixed it well. Finally, after the sample centrifuged, 5 mL of the leachate was transferred after centrifuging for 10 min at 4700 rpm for later detection.

The fluorescent polyamide laminates were prepared by a simple method. Firstly, the purified B, N–CDs was dissolved in 10 mL ethanol (18 mg mL−1). And 10 μL B, N–CDs ethanol solution was painted in a 6 mm diameter circle of polyamide laminate. Secondly, 5 μL of reaction solution containing MP at different concentration was painted in the dried fluorescence laminates. Finally, the imaging of the laminates was observed at the 365 nm of UV–vis light and were processed by Image J software.

The morphology details of particle size distribution and almost spherical shapes of B, N–CDs were revealed by the HRTEM. B, N–CDs were well dissolved in ethanol with good dispersion (Fig. 1 a). According to the Fig. S1a, the B, NCDs possessed a concentrated size distribution (3–4 nm) and the average diameter was 3.4 nm based on the calculation of weighted average. As the FT–IR result showed, the characteristic peaks 1110, 1160, 1353, 1446, 1583 and 1668 cm−1 were respectively attributed to CO, CC, CB, CH2, CC/CN and CO (Fig. 1b). In the XPS spectra of B, NCDs (Fig. 1c), the distinct signals at 191.88, 284.88, 399.88 and 531.88 eV were ascribed to B 1s, C 1s, N 1s and O 1s, respectively. For further observing, the high-resolution image of C 1s, B 1s and N 1s were separately subjected to peak fitting analysis (Fig. 1d–f.). As the Fig. 1d represented, B 1s was composed of BO (190.7 eV) and BC (191.1 eV) bonds [34]. The high-resolution spectrum of C 1 s (Fig. 1e) was revealed that there was C-B (283.6 eV), CO (284.7 eV), CC/CC (285.1 eV), NCC (285.7 eV) and CCO (288.1 eV) bonds. In addition, from the N 1s spectrum (Fig. 1f), the 399.5, 400.1 and 401.5 eV were respectively attributed to NH, polaron and bipolaron signals. As the result indicated, B, N–CDs surface exist plenty of functional group like CO, OH, BO, CB, NH, COOH, etc.

As the TEM image (Fig. 2 a) showed, CeO2 tended to be agglomerated together. In the high-resolution TEM image (Fig. 2b), CeO2 possessed near sphere shape with the 0.336 nm of interplanar spacing. According to the Fig. S1b, the average particle size of CeO2 was 3.7 nm based on the calculation of weighted average. For further stating the structure of the CeO2, XPS and X-RD were used. As the XPS spectra of CeO2 showed in Fig. S2, CeO2 clearly represented oxygen (O 1 s) and cerium (Ce 4d3/2, Ce 3d) signals. Because Cerium had various valences that showed many different distinct characteristic peaks. The high resolution of XPS of Ce 3d was ascribed to Ce3+ and Ce4+ ions. On the one hand, the signals of 885.4, 896.1 and 902.7 eV were belonged to the Ce3+ and the signals of 883.7, 881.5, 897.5, 900.1 907.2 and 915.9 eV were ascribed to the Ce4+ [35]. On the other hand, the signals of 881.5, 883.7, 885.4, 887.6, 896.1 and 897.5 eV belonged to the Ce 3d5/2 spin-orbit. And the signals of 900.1, 902.7, 907.2 and 915.9 eV belonged to the Ce 3d3/2 spin-orbit. As the X–RD (Fig. 2d) spectra showed, the as-prepared CeO2 perfectly matched with the X–RD pattern (JCPDS No. 65–5923) of CeO2 from X–RD database to prove the successful synthesis and character of polycrystalline [36].

CeO2 could decompose MP to p-NP which would cause to quench the fluorescence emission of B, N–CDs at 440 nm (Scheme. 1). Therefore, CeO2 and B, N–CDs were united to detect concentration of MP in the samples. For reaching a better fluorescence detection assay of B, N–CDs for p-NP, (a) pH, (b) solvent, (c) sensor concentrations, and (d) incubation time were optimized. As shown in Fig. S3a–c, the optimum detection conditions were established with 30 μL mL−1 of sensor adding volume, pH of water at 10. Also, the incubation time is important to the detection system. As the Fig. S3d represented, quenching rate of B, N–CDs trended to be steady after adding p-NP for 2 min. B, N–CDs presented the highest fluorescence intensity at 440 nm when the excitation wavelength was at 370 nm (Fig. 3 a). After uniting the UV–vis absorption spectra of p-NP with the fluorescence spectra of B, N–CDs, a perfect match of shadow was showed in the Fig. 3b which indicates the IFE effect at 400 nm. As the Fig. S4 showed, the fluorescence lifetime of B, NCDs (3.5 ns) had ignorable different with coexisting the 15 μM MP and B, NCDs (3.7 ns). Therefore, the result of lifetime scan proved that the B, NCDs fluorescence intensity was quenched by p-NP through the IFE mechanism rather than the PET mechanism or FRET effect. Furthermore, the effects of CeO2 and MP on the fluorescent intensity of B, NCDs were investigated. As shown in Fig. S5, CeO2 or MP had negligible effect on the fluorescent intensity. After the addition of CeO2 and MP simultaneously, the significant fluorescence decrease could be observed which attribute to the successful production of p-NP from MP.

After the present fluorescent detection systems was established, the quenching rate value was used to calculate the fluorescent intensity changes of B, N–CDs at 440 nm with MP at different concentration. The quenching rate (QR) of this detection system was calculated by the following Eq. (1).
(1)
QR =
(
F 0 − F
)
F 0
Respectively, C, F0 and F were the MP concentration, the fluorescent intensity of B, N–CDs in the absence and presence of MP at a certain concentration.

The B, N–CDs fluorescent intensity was gradually reduced through the IFE mechanism by p-NP which was decomposed from MP by CeO2 from 0 to 25 μM (Fig. 4 a). Besides, the emission of B, N–CDs was shifted to longer wavelength from 440 to 465 nm by the escalating concentration of MP. The B, N–CDs fluorescence quenching rate represented a high linear trend with the MP range from 0.1 to 15 μM, and the linearity (y = 0.04096*X + +0.05356, R2 = 0.9967) was observed at Fig. 4b. The limit of detection for MP was 0.1 μM, based on S/N > 3 (where N is the standard deviation). And the detection system was saturated more than 15 μM. The detection system had lower limit of detection and wider linear range which was comparable to the other OPs detection methods (Table. S1).

The proposed detection system was certain to meet with abundant interference substances in natural plant samples, which affected the accuracy of the detection result. Therefore, some common endogenous and exogenous interferences were chosen to test the anti-interference capability of the detection system. For testing the anti-interference ability of the detection system, common substance (Alanine, proline, serine, leucine, Cl−, SO4 2−) in the plant at 1.5 mM were added separately while MP was kept at 15 μM. As the Fig. 5 presented, only SO4 2− had a slight up-regulation effect on the detection systems. But considering that the concentration ratio between the substances and MP was 100 to 1, the up-regulation effects would be reduced in the real sample. Alanine, proline, serine and leucine are ubiquitous in natural plants and play an important role in their growth, which would possibly be an interference to affect the detection system. But in fact, we found that they did not affect the accurate quantification of the MP concentration on the detection system (Fig. 5). In modern world, different kinds of OPs were continually used to protect plants from worms and insects. Furthermore, it is important to detect MP in the presence of multiple pesticides. Five kinds of other OPs (paraoxon-ethyl, dichlorvos, fensulfothion, monocrotophos and malaoxon) at 15 μM were selected to test the anti-interference ability. The paraoxon-ethyl was considered as a high probability of interference for the detection system. The paraoxon-ethyl and MP are structural analog, and both could be decomposed into p-NP molecules. As the Fig. 5 showed, the column of ethyl-paraoxon with MP was slightly higher than the column of control. As the result showed, the detection system represented a high selectiveness ability, which was attributed from CeO2 accurately recognized the phosphate group and from B, N–CDs as a fluorescent probe detected MP specifically through the IFE mechanism.

The recoveries of the samples of Semen Coicis and Panax quinquefolius L. were showed in the Table 1 . The leachates from Panax quinquefolius L. contained lots of interferential components. However, the recoveries are in the range of 101.28–105.73%, and the detection systems represent a high repeatability. Water sources play an important role in human life. For lake water, there were some metabolites and excretion from animals and natural plants which would affect the MP detection. The result of water recovery was shown in Table 2 . The recoveries of tap water and lake water located in the range from 96.45 to 99.22% and 101.66 to 107.28%, respectively. The result showed a high repeatability with the RSD below 4%. As the tables showing and analysis above, our fast detection system showed a good recovery in the real sample detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual detection of MP by B, N–CDs fluorescent polyamide laminate
ce:section.ce:para:
A quick response, simple, high selectiveness and portable OPs detection fluorescent laminate was developed. B, N–CDs possessed a high solubility in the ethanol and high fluorescence intensity. Thus, ethanol was selected to accelerate the drying process. As the laminate images showed (Fig. 6
), B, N–CDs were successfully painted in the laminate of polyamide laminate and represented a blue color under the light resource of 365 nm UV–vis. After using the CeO2 to pretreat the MP samples, the B, N–CDs fluorescence was immediately quenched by p-NP at different concentration (1.5, 0.75, 0.375, 0.15, 0.075, 0.015 mM) and the change can be observed by the eyes under the 365 nm ultraviolet light (Fig. 6a). As the Fig. 6b showed, 1.5 mM of other pesticides, for example ethyl-paraoxon (EP), dichlorvos (Dich.), fensulfothion (Fen.), monocrotophos (Mono.) and malaoxon (Mala.), were used to contrast with MP signal on the fluorescence laminates. Under the 365 nm ultraviolet light, the B, N–CDs fluorescence laminate represented a high selectiveness for MP detection and this detection system can be activated by the pesticide which possess the structure of p-NP. Meanwhile, the imaging of these laminates was processed by Image J software for fluorescence intensity analyzing. The images in Fig. 6a exhibited the fluorescence intensity was gradually quenched when the MP concentration ranged from 0.015 to 1.5 mM. Furthermore, the other pesticides were selected to investigate the specificity of the proposed method. Most pesticide had negligible effect on the fluorescence intensity except for EP with very weak effect due to its structural analogue of MP.

In this work, we combined fluorescent probe with inorganic catalyst together to set up the MP detection system based on the fluorescent and visual detection through the IFE. CeO2 was applied as an effective catalyst to deal with the parent compound, and B, N–CDs were used as the fluorescent probe to detect the target molecules from the reaction product. The detection system provides a thinking way to detect the target indirectly but still to maintain its accuracy and repeatability. This paper shows that the specificity and accuracy of the analytical method for MP detection could be greatly improved by the combination of fluorescent and visual signals in real sample analysis. The proposed detection study proved that this visual and fluorescence method could be used to be as an easy, convenient and reliable detection system for MP detection.

Mai Luo: Investigation, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Jinchao Wei: Methodology, Visualization, Writing - review & editing. Yunyang Zhao: Data curation. Yuzhou Sun: Investigation. Hengxing Liang: Resources, Formal analysis. Shuangpeng Wang: Resources, Supervision. Peng Li: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support from The Science and Technology Development Fund, Macau SAR (File no. 162/2017/A3), Guangxi Innovation-driven Development Special Foundation Project (File no. GuiKe AA18118049), National Science and Technology Major Projects for “Major New Drugs Innovation and Development” (File no. 2018ZX09201018-023), and the Research Committee of the University of Macau (File no. MYRG2018-00239-ICMS).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.microc.2019.104547.
